nativeRange.habitat: Shallow estuarine waters and intertidal mudflats; commonly associated with dead oyster shells and oyster beds

diet.notes: Diet should include vegetable matter; frozen and dried foods containing marine algae and blue-green algae are generally accepted. May also pick at small invertebrates opportunistically.

summary: The Brachiosaurus Blenny (Omobranchus anolius) is a small Australian blenny native to estuarine and intertidal habitats, recognised by its pronounced fleshy crest and elaborate dorsal fin. It tends to perch in oyster shells and rubble and is generally considered semi-aggressive toward similarly shaped fish.
careNotes: The Brachiosaurus Blenny is endemic to eastern Australia, ranging from the Gulf of Carpentaria to Spencer Gulf. In the wild it tends to inhabit dead oyster shells and shallow estuarine rubble, and aquariums are generally advised to replicate this with plenty of scattered rock, empty shells, and hiding spots. Males are distinguished by a pronounced fleshy crest and elaborate dorsal fin. The species has been reported to tolerate a relatively wide and cool temperature range, down to about 16°C, making it well suited to cooler-running systems. In reef aquariums, it has been noted to pick at clam mantles and fleshy large-polyp stony corals when underfed, so keeping it well supplied with algae-based foods is commonly recommended. Like most blennies, it has been known to jump from open aquariums, and a tight-fitting lid is strongly advised.
breedingNotes: The Brachiosaurus Blenny is oviparous and breeds in distinct pairs. Eggs are demersal (laid on a surface and adhesive) and larvae are planktonic in coastal waters. Captive breeding has not been widely reported in the hobby, though the species' pairing behavior and preference for sheltered cavities such as empty shells suggest that in-tank spawning may be feasible given suitable conditions.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (FishBase: 7.5 cm TL vs LiveAquaria: 3.5 in / 8.89 cm — averaged to 8.2 cm max), temperature range (FishBase: 14.8–25.2°C vs LiveAquaria: 64–77°F / 17.8–25°C — averaged to min 16°C / max 25°C), and native range (confirmed eastern Australia, Gulf of Carpentaria to Spencer Gulf). fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous, breeding in distinct pairs. Eggs are demersal and adhesive; larvae are planktonic. Captive breeding has not been widely reported.
